title: Sāmāyika Pārvānī — Verses & Sūtra for Concluding the Sāmāyika
summary: This is the closing recitation that a lay practitioner says to wind up a sāmāyika — the roughly 48-minute vow of sitting in equanimity, free of harmful activity. In a short string of verses and a prose confession it does three things: it asks and grants forgiveness to all living beings, it admits any mistakes made while keeping the vow, and it bows to the Jinas before releasing the vow. If you are new, think of it as the respectful 'sign-off' that seals the practice and returns the practitioner to ordinary life with a clean heart.
ritualPlacement: Sāmāyika Pārvānī belongs to the sixth Āvaśyaka, Paccakkhāṇa (pratyākhyāna — the disposition of the vow), and specifically performs the *pāraṇā* (formal conclusion) of the sāmāyika. In the Devasi (Daivasika) pratikramaṇa it comes near the very end, after the sajjhāya cluster and the 'conclude the sāmāyika' vidhi, just before the colophon. The Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) book teaches this 'conclude a sāmāyika' procedure once (book pp.41–44); other observances reuse it by reference. Accordingly it is also used in the Deśāvagāśika (the tenth lay vow) appendix, where the same sāmāyika frame is applied with the standard word-substitution — so do not treat the Deśāvagāśika version as a separate composition.
authorAndSource: This is a traditional liturgical compilation, not the work of one named author; the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa) book *Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit* is the golden source for its exact wording. Its component gāthās are drawn from the wider Śvetāmbara stock: the equanimity-praise verses 'Sāmāiya vaya jutto…' and 'Sāmāiyammi u kae… samaṇo iva sāvao havai' belong to the Āvaśyaka Niryukti / Sāmāyika-Āvaśyaka tradition, and the closing 'Tameva saccaṁ nissaṅkaṁ…' is a well-known Āvaśyaka-tradition affirmation of the Jina's word; the final 'Sarvamaṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ… jainaṁ jayati śāsanaṁ' is a traditional maṅgala (auspicious benediction). Exact authorship and redaction of the individual gāthās are not fixed by the book itself (see notes).
structureArc: The recitation runs in two movements. The seven opening gāthās (vv.1–7) carry the emotional and doctrinal arc: v.1 makes the threefold *micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ* — confessing all sin bound by mind, speech, and body; v.2 widens this to universal forgiveness, naming all souls wandering the fourteen-*rajju* universe bound by karma, asking their pardon and granting it; v.3 closes the forgiveness movement by declaring that, having forgiven and been forgiven by the six classes of beings, no enmity remains in a pure mind; vv.4, 6 and 7 then extol the worth of sāmāyika itself — v.4 says even one who daily gives away a lakh of gold cannot equal the merit of one sāmāyika, v.6 that as long as the mind stays joined to the vow it severs inauspicious karma 'as many times as sāmāyika is done', and v.7 that in sāmāyika the householder 'becomes like a monk', so one should do it often; v.5 interposes a small purificatory note, atoning for anything misspoken through negligence within the Jina's teaching. The second movement is the prose-and-verse Sūtra proper, numbered (1)–(9): (1) declares the vow *touched, kept, completed, carried through, observed by the proper vidhi* and confesses any *avidhi* / *āśātanā*; (2) confesses disrespect to study-implements (writing-board, manuscript, rosary, stand) and *jñāna-dravya*; (3)–(6) offer threefold (*trikāla*) homage in turn to the four-fold Saṅgha across the two-and-a-half continents, the twenty living *viharamāna* Jinas headed by Sīmandhara, the past/future/present twenty-four-fold sets (cauvīsī), and the four eternal (*śāśvata*) Jinas Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vāriṣeṇa; (7) confesses the thirty-two faults of sāmāyika (ten of mind, ten of speech, twelve of body); (8) affirms 'that alone is true which the Jinas proclaimed', accepting the true and disowning the false with *micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ*; and (9) seals the whole with the *sarvamaṅgala* benediction, 'Victory to the Jina's teaching.'
recurringImagery: Two refrains stitch the whole recitation together and should be heard as structural pillars rather than repetition. The first is *micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ* ('may my misdeed be in vain / undone') — the Jain formula of retraction that recurs in vv.1 and 5 and in Sūtra items (1), (2), (7) and (8); each time it converts a recognized fault into a renounced one. The second is the *trikāla-vandanā* refrain 'may my threefold (past-present-future) homage be' that paces items (3)–(6), turning the conclusion into an act of devotion that reaches every Jina across time and space. Around these runs the *kṣamā / khāmaṇā* (mutual-forgiveness) motif of vv.2–3 — the practitioner forgives and seeks forgiveness of all beings so that the equanimity practiced in sāmāyika is carried outward into relationship — and the 'incomparable worth of sāmāyika' motif of vv.4, 6–7, which justifies the whole act by measuring a single equanimity-session against gold, against severed karma, and against monastic life itself.
keyThemes: Threefold confession via micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ
The supreme merit of sāmāyika over worldly giving
Equanimity making the layman 'like a monk'
Trikāla homage to Jinas across time and the cosmos
Affirmation of the Jina's word as the sole truth
Closing maṅgala — victory to the Jina-śāsana

glossary.definition: The vow of equanimity: a fixed period (about 48 minutes / two ghaṭikā) of renouncing all harmful activity and resting the mind in balance; the first of the six Āvaśyakas.
The formal act of releasing or 'breaking' a completed vow; here, the procedure for concluding the sāmāyika.
'May my misdeed be undone/in vain' — the standard Jain formula of confession and retraction of a fault.
Mutual forgiveness: asking pardon of all beings for harm done and granting pardon in return, so no enmity (vaira) remains.
The six classes of living beings — earth-, water-, fire-, air-bodied, plant life, and mobile (trasa) beings — toward all of whom forgiveness is sought.
The fourteen-rajju vertical span of the inhabited universe (loka), through which karmically bound souls transmigrate.
Threefold homage offered for the past, present, and future — the devotional refrain that paces the closing salutations.
The twenty Tīrthaṅkaras presently living and teaching in other regions (notably Sīmandhara-svāmī), distinct from the twenty-four of our era.
The four eternal Jinas — Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vāriṣeṇa — whose images are held to exist perpetually.
A set of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras; the verse salutes the past (atīta), future (anāgata), and present (vartamāna) sets.
The 'two-and-a-half continents' — the region of the middle world inhabited by human beings, hence by the four-fold Saṅgha.
Implements and materials dedicated to sacred knowledge — manuscripts, writing-boards, stands, rosaries — disrespect to which is confessed.
Disrespect, irreverence, or violation of decorum toward what is sacred (the guru's seat, study-implements, the vow itself).
The prescribed correct procedure (vidhi) and any lapse from it (avidhi); the conclusion confesses any avidhi committed.
Renunciation or vow-resolution — the sixth Āvaśyaka, under which the sāmāyika is taken and concluded.
The thirty-two faults of sāmāyika — ten of mind, ten of speech, twelve of body — confessed in Sūtra item (7).
An auspicious benediction; the closing 'Sarvamaṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ…' seals the recitation with praise of the Jina-śāsana.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a heartfelt closing ritual — first forgiving and seeking forgiveness from all beings (vv.1–3), then admitting any slips in keeping the vow (Sūtra 1–2, 7), then bowing to the Jinas (Sūtra 3–6) and ending on an auspicious note (Sūtra 9). An advanced practitioner should note the deliberate movement through the sixth Āvaśyaka — confession, then trikāla devotion, then the doctrinal affirmation 'Tameva saccaṁ' — and read the equanimity-praise gāthās (vv.4, 6–7) as the doctrinal warrant for why the sāmāyika just performed was worth doing.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Authoritative verbatim wording, ritual placement, and the shared conclude-sāmāyika / Deśāvagāśika reuse model.

sources.title: Āvaśyaka Niryukti (Sāmāyika-Āvaśyaka section)
sources.consultedFor: Tradition-source of the equanimity-praise gāthās ('Sāmāiya vaya jutto', 'Sāmāiyammi u kae') and the six-Āvaśyaka framework.

notes: Placement and role (sixth Āvaśyaka pāraṇā of the sāmāyika; use in Devasi and Deśāvagāśika) are attested in the tradition knowledge base and the source book. The text shows the book's two-block layout — seven Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa gāthās followed by a separately numbered (1)–(9) prose-and-verse Sūtra — and its orthography (e.g. 'અતિયાર'-type spellings, mixed conjuncts) should be preserved verbatim per the book's fidelity rules. Per-gāthā authorship is given conservatively: the equanimity verses are widely transmitted in the Āvaśyaka Niryukti / Sāmāyika tradition and 'Tameva saccaṁ' is a standard Āvaśyaka affirmation, but the source book does not itself assign authors, and 'Sarvamaṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ' is a traditional maṅgala of uncertain single origin — these attributions are therefore marked as tradition-level, not pinned to a critical edition. The cited URLs point to the general Jain eLibrary repository rather than to a specific verified page; treat exact gāthā numbering in external editions as approximate.
